events.text: The patient is a female named Margaret Chen, born in 1977. She works as a high school biology teacher and is a non-smoker with no significant toxic exposures. She has a history of hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine and no prior hematologic conditions.

At age 48 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), NOS (ICD-O-3 histology code 9861/3), arising de novo without prior MDS or antecedent hematologic disorder. Site: bone marrow and peripheral blood (ICD-O-3 site code C42.1). AJCC staging not applicable for leukemia. Summary stage: systemic/disseminated. Presenting clinical features: WBC 87,400/uL with circulating blasts (~40%), hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL, platelets 32,000/uL. Gingival hyperplasia with spontaneous bleeding noted on oral examination. Bone marrow biopsy showed hypercellular marrow (95%) with 72% myeloblasts. Auer rods identified on peripheral smear. Immunophenotype: CD34+, CD117+, CD13+, CD33+, MPO+, HLA-DR+, CD7 dim, CD19-, CD3-. Cytogenetics: 46,XX, normal karyotype (NK-AML). FLT3-ITD positive with allelic ratio 0.68 (high). NPM1 mutation status: negative. CEBPA: not mutated. IDH1/IDH2: negative. ELN 2022 Risk Classification: Adverse (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio with normal karyotype in NPM1-unmutated setting).

At age 48 years, the patient had a bone marrow biopsy and aspirate (right posterior iliac crest). Pathology result: hypercellular bone marrow (estimated cellularity 95%) with sheets of myeloblasts comprising 72% of total nucleated cells. Blasts show large nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and moderate amounts of basophilic cytoplasm. Auer rods present. Flow cytometry immunophenotyping: blast population CD45-dim, CD34+, CD117+, CD33+, CD13+, MPO+, HLA-DR+, CD7 dim positive, CD19 negative, CD3 negative, CD56 negative, CD14 negative, CD64 negative, consistent with AML, not otherwise specified. Cytogenetics by conventional karyotype: 46,XX [20/20 metaphases analyzed], normal female karyotype. FISH panel: negative for t(8;21), inv(16), t(15;17). Molecular studies sent separately.

At age 48 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for acute myeloid leukemia based on a specimen obtained from bone marrow aspirate at diagnosis. A comprehensive myeloid panel (523-gene panel) showed the following findings: FLT3-ITD insertion (exon 14, 33 bp insertion, allelic ratio 0.68, VAF 40.2%) - clinically actionable, associated with adverse prognosis; DNMT3A p.R882H mutation (VAF 48.1%) - pathogenic, co-mutation commonly seen with FLT3-ITD; RUNX1 p.S291fs*16 frameshift mutation (VAF 43.7%) - pathogenic, associated with adverse ELN risk; TET2 p.Q916* nonsense mutation (VAF 22.4%) - pathogenic, likely subclonal; WT1 p.R462Q mutation (VAF 18.9%) - variant of uncertain significance, associated with AML; PTPN11 p.E76K mutation (VAF 8.3%) - likely pathogenic, RAS pathway activation; no FLT3-TKD (D835) mutation detected; no NPM1 mutation; no CEBPA mutation; no IDH1/IDH2 mutation; no TP53 mutation; no ASXL1 mutation; no NRAS/KRAS mutations; no KIT mutation. Copy number: no significant copy number alterations detected. Fusions: no pathognomonic fusions detected (BCR-ABL1, PML-RARA, RUNX1-RUNX1T1, CBFB-MYH11 all negative). Summary: high-risk AML with FLT3-ITD (high allelic ratio), DNMT3A, RUNX1, and TET2 co-mutations. ELN 2022 Adverse risk confirmed.

At age 48 years, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (staging/pre-treatment). Findings: no mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Mild splenomegaly with spleen measuring 14.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension. Liver is mildly enlarged at 17.1 cm. No hepatic lesions. No lymphadenopathy in the abdomen or pelvis. No lytic or blastic osseous lesions. Small bilateral pleural effusions. Impression: Splenomegaly and hepatomegaly consistent with leukemic infiltration. No discrete tumor masses. No evidence of CNS involvement on brain MRI (performed separately): no leptomeningeal enhancement, no parenchymal lesions.

At age 48 years, the patient had an initial oncology consultation and treatment planning note. The patient presented with a 3-week history of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, gingival bleeding, and a 12-pound unintentional weight loss. Physical examination showed pallor, petechiae on bilateral lower extremities, gingival hyperplasia with active oozing, and a palpable spleen tip 4 cm below the left costal margin. ECOG performance status: 1. She was admitted to the inpatient hematology/oncology unit for induction chemotherapy. Transfusion support initiated: 2 units pRBC for hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL, platelets transfused for count <10,000/uL. Echocardiogram obtained pre-chemotherapy: LVEF 62%, no significant valvular disease. Plan: proceed with 7+3 induction chemotherapy (cytarabine + daunorubicin) plus midostaurin per the RATIFY protocol. Allogeneic SCT to be planned in first CR given adverse ELN risk. HLA typing sent on patient and siblings (one full sibling available). Unrelated donor search initiated concurrently.

At age 48 years, the patient received induction chemotherapy for AML with curative intent: cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day continuous infusion on days 1-7 AND daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV on days 1-3 (7+3 regimen) PLUS midostaurin 50 mg PO twice daily on days 8-21 (FLT3 inhibitor, added per RATIFY trial data). Anti-emetics: ondansetron, dexamethasone. Tumor lysis prophylaxis: allopurinol, aggressive IV hydration. Growth factor support withheld per standard induction protocol. The patient was hospitalized throughout induction.

At age 48 years (during induction, approximately day 10), the patient developed febrile neutropenia (temperature 38.9 degrees C, ANC 0/uL). Grade 3 adverse event. Blood cultures drawn; empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics initiated with cefepime 2g IV q8h. Oral mucositis grade 2 also present, managed with magic mouthwash and morphine PCA for pain control. CT sinuses and chest obtained: bilateral lower lobe consolidations concerning for early pneumonia. Antifungal coverage added: micafungin 150 mg IV daily. The patient defervesced after 5 days of antibiotics. No bacteremia identified. Presumed diagnosis: pneumonia (possibly fungal). Total antibiotic course: 14 days.

At age 48 years (during induction, approximately day 14), the patient developed grade 2 nausea and vomiting despite ondansetron prophylaxis, attributed to midostaurin. Prochlorperazine added with improvement. Midostaurin continued at full dose. Additionally, grade 1 transaminase elevation noted (ALT 58 U/L, AST 45 U/L), monitored and resolved without intervention.

At age 48 years (day 14 post-induction start), the patient had an inpatient oncology assessment. CBC showed WBC 0.2 k/uL (nadir, ANC 0/uL), hemoglobin 8.4 g/uL (transfusion-supported), platelets 9,000/uL (transfusion-supported). Patient remained afebrile on antibiotics. Bone marrow biopsy deferred until day 28 per protocol. Midostaurin continued through day 21. Transfusion requirements: 6 units pRBC and 12 units platelets during induction hospitalization. No signs of leukostasis. Status: active cancer treatment in progress, aplasia phase.

At age 48 years (day 28 post-induction), the patient had a bone marrow biopsy and aspirate for response assessment. Pathology result: hypocellular bone marrow (estimated cellularity 10-15%) with trilineage hematopoiesis emerging. Myeloblasts: 3% of total nucleated cells (complete remission criteria met: <5% blasts). No Auer rods identified. Flow cytometry for MRD: no immunophenotypically aberrant blast population detected above the threshold of 0.01% (MRD negative by 8-color flow cytometry). Cytogenetics: 46,XX [15/20 metaphases; 5 metaphases inadequate], normal karyotype. FLT3-ITD by PCR: not detected. DNMT3A and RUNX1 mutations: not detected by sensitive sequencing at this sensitivity level. Assessment: morphologic complete remission (CR) with MRD negativity by flow cytometry.

At age 48 years (approximately 5 weeks after diagnosis), the patient had an oncology assessment post-induction. The patient achieved morphologic complete remission (CR) with MRD negativity after a single induction cycle. CBC recovering: WBC 3.1 k/uL, hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, platelets 88,000/uL. Patient is being discharged from hospital. Performance status ECOG 1, improving. Plan: proceed directly to allogeneic stem cell transplant given adverse-risk disease (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, RUNX1 mutation, DNMT3A mutation). No consolidation chemotherapy planned prior to transplant given expedited transplant timeline. HLA typing results reviewed: sibling is a 7/10 HLA match (not suitable). Unrelated donor search identifies a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor (MUD). Transplant center consultation confirmed. Target transplant in 6-8 weeks. Midostaurin maintenance bridge therapy to be considered.

At age 48 years (pre-transplant evaluation, approximately 6 weeks post-diagnosis), the patient had a PET-CT scan of the whole body (pre-transplant restaging). Findings: no FDG-avid lesions in the neck, chest, abdomen, or pelvis. No lymphadenopathy. Spleen now measures 11.8 cm (previously 14.2 cm, improved). Liver size normal at 15.3 cm. No bone lesions. Impression: complete metabolic response, no evidence of residual or extramedullary disease. Consistent with complete remission.

At age 48 years (approximately 7 weeks post-diagnosis), the patient had a pre-transplant evaluation visit at the transplant center. Detailed history and physical: ECOG PS 0-1. No active infections. No significant organ dysfunction. Cardiac evaluation: LVEF 60% on repeat echo (stable). Pulmonary function tests: FEV1 88% predicted, DLCO 82% predicted (adequate for transplant). Renal function: creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, eGFR >90. Hepatic function: transaminases normal. Dental clearance obtained. Caregiver support confirmed (husband is primary caregiver). Psychosocial evaluation completed. Consent obtained for matched unrelated donor (MUD) allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (alloHSCT) with myeloablative conditioning. Donor: 10/10 HLA-matched (A, B, C, DRB1, DQB1) unrelated male donor, age 32, CMV seropositive. Patient is CMV seropositive. Plan: fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4) myeloablative conditioning with tacrolimus/methotrexate GVHD prophylaxis.

At age 48 years (transplant conditioning, approximately day -7 to day 0 of transplant), the patient received myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine 40 mg/m2 IV daily on days -6 through -3 AND busulfan 3.2 mg/kg IV daily on days -6 through -3 (FluBu4, pharmacokinetically dosed with target AUC 4000-5000 umol*min/L/day). Anti-seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam administered during busulfan. GVHD prophylaxis: tacrolimus 0.03 mg/kg/day continuous IV infusion starting day -3, target trough 5-15 ng/mL, AND methotrexate 15 mg/m2 IV on day +1, then 10 mg/m2 on days +3, +6, +11. CMV prophylaxis: letermovir 480 mg daily starting day +1. Antifungal prophylaxis: micafungin. PJP prophylaxis: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (held during conditioning, restarted post-engraftment). Day 0: infusion of 10/10 HLA-MUD peripheral blood stem cells (CD34+ cell dose 5.2 x 10^6/kg).

At age 48 years (post-transplant, approximately day +8), the patient developed grade 3 mucositis with inability to tolerate oral intake, requiring total parenteral nutrition (TPN) and IV morphine PCA. Also developed grade 2 nausea/vomiting and grade 1 diarrhea. Managed with aggressive supportive care. Mucositis gradually improved by day +18. Additionally, elevated tacrolimus level (25 ng/mL on day +10) required dose reduction; renal function transiently worsened (creatinine 1.4 mg/dL from baseline 0.8 mg/dL). Dose adjusted, levels corrected, creatinine returned to 1.0 mg/dL by day +20.

At age 48 years (post-transplant day +30), the patient had a bone marrow biopsy and aspirate for engraftment and response assessment. Pathology result: normocellular bone marrow (cellularity 50-60%) with trilineage hematopoiesis. Myeloblasts: <1% (within normal limits). No morphologic evidence of AML. Donor chimerism by STR analysis: total chimerism 98% donor (CD3+ T cells: 92% donor; CD33+ myeloid cells: 99% donor). MRD by flow cytometry: negative (no immunophenotypically aberrant cells detected above 0.01% threshold). Cytogenetics: 46,XY [donor karyotype, 20/20 metaphases] - confirms complete donor chimerism. Assessment: engraftment confirmed with complete donor chimerism and morphologic remission.

At age 48 years (post-transplant day +45 clinic visit), the patient had an oncology and transplant assessment. The patient was discharged from hospital on day +28. Currently ECOG PS 1. CBC: WBC 4.8 k/uL, ANC 2,800/uL, hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL, platelets 112,000/uL - counts fully recovered. Tacrolimus trough level 9.2 ng/mL (therapeutic). No signs of acute GVHD at this visit (skin, GI, liver - all normal). CMV PCR: undetectable on letermovir prophylaxis. Epstein-Barr virus PCR: undetectable. No infections. Continues on tacrolimus, letermovir, acyclovir, fluconazole, TMP-SMX (restarted). Cancer status: in complete remission, MRD negative. Plan: gradual tacrolimus taper to begin at approximately day +100 if no GVHD.

At age 48 years (post-transplant day +90, approximately 5 months post-diagnosis), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (routine post-transplant surveillance). Findings: no lymphadenopathy. Spleen normal size at 10.5 cm. Liver normal size, no lesions. No new pulmonary infiltrates. Mild ground-glass opacities in the right lower lobe, likely resolving infection vs. engraftment syndrome (clinically asymptomatic, stable to prior imaging). No evidence of extramedullary disease. Impression: no evidence of AML recurrence. Mild residual pulmonary findings, clinically insignificant.

At age 48 years (post-transplant day +100), the patient had a transplant and oncology follow-up. The patient developed new onset of mild skin changes: scattered erythematous papules on bilateral forearms and dorsal hands, covering approximately 10% body surface area. No oral mucosal involvement at this time. GI: 1-2 loose stools per day (baseline), no blood. Liver function tests: total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, ALT 48 U/L, AST 38 U/L (mildly elevated). Skin biopsy obtained. Assessment: clinical picture consistent with chronic GVHD (cGVHD), mild-moderate severity per NIH consensus criteria, skin and possibly GI involvement (overall NIH global severity score: mild). Tacrolimus taper deferred. Topical corticosteroids initiated for skin GVHD (clobetasol ointment). Cancer status: complete remission maintained.

At age 48 years (post-transplant day +102), the patient had a skin punch biopsy of right forearm papule for evaluation of GVHD. Pathology result: lichenoid tissue reaction with basal layer vacuolization, dyskeratotic keratinocytes, and lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermal-epidermal junction. Findings are consistent with chronic graft-versus-host disease, lichenoid type (NIH grade 2). No evidence of malignancy or infection. Comment: morphologic features are characteristic of cGVHD and correlate with clinical presentation.

At age 48 years (post-transplant, days +100 through +180), the patient experienced mild chronic GVHD affecting skin (lichenoid changes, 15% BSA involvement, NIH score 2) and gastrointestinal tract (persistent loose stools 2-3 per day, mild cramping, no bleeding, NIH score 1). Managed with topical clobetasol to skin lesions (significant improvement), oral budesonide 3 mg TID for GI symptoms, and continuation of tacrolimus at therapeutic doses. Overall NIH global cGVHD score: mild. No requirement for systemic high-dose corticosteroids. Oral mucosa: mild xerostomia noted, managed with saliva substitutes.

At age 48 years (post-transplant day +180, approximately 8 months post-diagnosis), the patient had a transplant and oncology follow-up. CBC: WBC 5.9 k/uL, ANC 3,600/uL, hemoglobin 12.3 g/dL, platelets 178,000/uL - all within normal limits. ECOG PS 0-1. Skin GVHD: markedly improved on topical corticosteroids, now <5% BSA involvement, no active papules. GI GVHD: loose stools reduced to 1-2 per day on budesonide. Tacrolimus trough 8.1 ng/mL. Donor chimerism (peripheral blood): 99% donor (total), 97% CD3+ T cells - excellent. CMV PCR: undetectable. No active infections. Cancer status: complete remission, no evidence of disease. Plan: continue slow tacrolimus taper over next 6 months targeting discontinuation by day +365 if cGVHD remains mild/controlled.

At age 48 years (post-transplant day +180), the patient had a bone marrow biopsy and aspirate for 6-month surveillance. Pathology result: normocellular bone marrow with trilineage hematopoiesis. Myeloblasts: <2% (within normal limits). No morphologic evidence of AML. Donor chimerism by STR: 99.5% donor (total), 98% CD3+ T cells, 100% CD33+ myeloid cells. MRD by flow cytometry: negative (below 0.01% threshold). Molecular testing: FLT3-ITD by quantitative PCR - not detected. DNMT3A R882H by ddPCR - not detected. Assessment: durable complete remission with full donor chimerism maintained at 6 months post-transplant.

At age 49 years (post-transplant approximately day +365, 1 year post-transplant), the patient had a PET-CT scan of the whole body (1-year post-transplant restaging). Findings: no FDG-avid lesions. No lymphadenopathy. Spleen: 10.2 cm (normal). Liver: normal size, no lesions. Lungs: clear, previously noted ground-glass opacities have completely resolved. No evidence of extramedullary disease. Impression: complete remission maintained at 1 year post-allogeneic SCT. No evidence of AML recurrence.

At age 49 years (post-transplant day +365, 1-year anniversary), the patient had a 1-year post-transplant comprehensive evaluation. The patient is doing well. ECOG PS 0. Returns to part-time teaching. Skin GVHD: in complete remission on low-dose topical corticosteroids (now down to over-the-counter hydrocortisone). GI cGVHD: resolved, budesonide successfully discontinued at month 9. Tacrolimus successfully tapered and discontinued at month 11 without GVHD flare. CBC: WBC 6.2 k/uL, Hgb 13.1 g/dL, Plt 210,000/uL. Comprehensive metabolic panel: within normal limits. Tacrolimus discontinued. All prophylactic antimicrobials discontinued per protocol. Bone marrow biopsy (see pathology report): no evidence of AML, full donor chimerism. Cancer status: morphologic and molecular complete remission. MRD negative. Plan: clinic follow-up every 3 months for year 2, bone marrow biopsy at 18 months if clinically indicated. Patient educated regarding signs of relapse and late effects of transplant.

At age 49 years (post-transplant day +365), the patient had a bone marrow biopsy and aspirate for 12-month surveillance. Pathology result: normocellular bone marrow (cellularity 40-50%) with normal trilineage hematopoiesis. Myeloblasts: 1% (within normal limits). No Auer rods. No morphologic evidence of AML. Donor chimerism by STR: 99.8% donor (total). MRD by 10-color flow cytometry: negative. Molecular: FLT3-ITD by quantitative PCR - not detected (sensitivity 0.001%). DNMT3A R882H by ddPCR - not detected. RUNX1 frameshift by NGS deep sequencing - not detected. Assessment: sustained complete molecular remission at 1 year post-allogeneic SCT, full donor chimerism.

At age 49 years (approximately 18 months post-transplant), the patient had a routine oncology and transplant follow-up. No new symptoms. ECOG PS 0. Returned to full-time teaching. Skin: no active GVHD lesions. GI: normal bowel habits. No medications except levothyroxine (pre-existing hypothyroidism) and daily aspirin. CBC: WBC 5.8 k/uL, Hgb 13.5 g/dL, Plt 195,000/uL. Comprehensive metabolic panel: ALT 22 U/L (normal), creatinine 0.9 mg/dL. Peripheral blood chimerism: 99.5% donor. No evidence of relapse clinically or by peripheral blood assessment. CMV, EBV: undetectable. Cancer status: complete remission, no evidence of disease. Patient counseled regarding long-term survivorship: secondary malignancy screening, vaccination schedule (live vaccines deferred until 2 years post-transplant), bone health (DEXA scan ordered), ophthalmology referral for cataract surveillance (busulfan exposure). Quality of life: good. Plan: continue surveillance every 3 months.

documents.text:
HEMATOPATHOLOGY REPORT
BONE MARROW BIOPSY AND ASPIRATE — POST-TRANSPLANT ENGRAFTMENT AND RESPONSE ASSESSMENT

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SPECIMEN INFORMATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Patient Name: Margaret Chen
Date of Birth: 1977
Medical Record Number: [on file]
Attending Physician: Hematology/Oncology / Bone Marrow Transplant Service
Ordering Physician: BMT Attending
Clinical Indication: Day +30 post-allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (alloHSCT) — engraftment assessment and response evaluation for known acute myeloid leukemia (AML), de novo, with FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, DNMT3A R882H, RUNX1 frameshift, ELN 2022 Adverse risk. Prior induction with 7+3 plus midostaurin; achieved morphologic CR with MRD negativity prior to transplant. Day 0: 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor (MUD) peripheral blood stem cell infusion.
Specimen ID: [Accession number on file]
Date of Procedure: Day +30 post-transplant

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TYPE OF SPECIMEN
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1. Bone marrow core biopsy (right posterior iliac crest)
2. Bone marrow aspirate (right posterior iliac crest)
3. Peripheral blood (submitted for donor chimerism studies)

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
GROSS PATHOLOGY DESCRIPTION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Specimen 1 — Core Biopsy:
Received in formalin, labeled with patient name and medical record number, is a single intact cylindrical core of bone marrow biopsy measuring 2.0 cm in length and approximately 0.2 cm in diameter. The specimen has a tan-pink, slightly trabecular cut surface. The cortical bone margin is present at one end. The specimen is entirely submitted for processing and decalcification followed by paraffin embedding. Sections stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), periodic acid-Schiff (PAS), reticulin silver stain, and Giemsa.

Specimen 2 — Aspirate Smears:
Received are 6 Wright-Giemsa-stained aspirate smears, labeled with the patient name and date. Spicules are present and adequate for assessment. Cellularity is adequate for differential counting. 500-cell differential performed on the most representative smear.

Specimen 3 — Peripheral Blood:
Received in EDTA tube and processed for concurrent chimerism studies (STR-based short tandem repeat analysis). Mononuclear cell fractionation performed for lineage-specific chimerism analysis.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I. MORPHOLOGY — BONE MARROW CORE BIOPSY

Overall Cellularity:
The bone marrow core biopsy demonstrates normocellularity with an estimated cellularity of 50–60%, appropriate for the patient's age and consistent with early engraftment in the post-transplant setting. This represents a substantial increase from the hypocellular marrow (10–15%) noted on the day +28 post-induction biopsy and reflects recovery of hematopoiesis following myeloablative conditioning (fludarabine/busulfan, FluBu4) and stem cell engraftment.

Hematopoietic Elements:
Trilineage hematopoiesis is present and morphologically maturing. All three lineages show evidence of orderly maturation.

- Erythroid precursors: Present in adequate numbers, arranged in normal erythroid islands. Normoblastic maturation with late-stage erythroblasts present. No megaloblastic change. No ringed sideroblasts identified on iron stain (results appended).

- Myeloid precursors: Present across all stages of maturation including myeloblasts, promyelocytes, myelocytes, metamyelocytes, and band/segmented neutrophils. The myeloid-to-erythroid (M:E) ratio is approximately 2.5:1, within normal limits. Eosinophilic precursors present in normal proportions.

- Megakaryocytes: Present, adequate in number, with normal lobulated morphology. No hypolobulated or micromegakaryocyte forms identified. Platelet budding apparent.

Blast Count:
Myeloblasts account for less than 1% of nucleated marrow cells (estimated <1% on core biopsy, confirmed on aspirate differential — see below). No Auer rods are identified. No clusters or aggregates of blasts are present. This is well below the 5% threshold for morphologic complete remission.

Stromal Elements:
Reticulin stain demonstrates MF-0 to MF-1 (mild, focal reticulin fibrosis without collagen formation), within the expected range following myeloablative conditioning and early engraftment. No significant increase in reticulin fibrosis. Bone trabeculae are intact without evidence of necrosis.

No evidence of infiltration by large cell lymphoma, plasma cell neoplasm, or other hematologic malignancy. No granulomas identified.

II. MORPHOLOGY — BONE MARROW ASPIRATE

The aspirate smears are adequate with multiple spicules. A 500-cell differential was performed:

  Myeloblasts:                0.8%
  Promyelocytes:              3.2%
  Myelocytes:                 9.4%
  Metamyelocytes:             8.6%
  Band forms:                 14.1%
  Segmented neutrophils:      18.8%
  Eosinophils (all stages):   2.4%
  Basophils:                  0.2%
  Monocytes:                  3.0%
  Lymphocytes:                8.7%
  Plasma cells:               0.4%
  Erythroid precursors:       30.4%
  (proerythroblasts/basophilic/polychromatophilic/orthochromatophilic)

Total blasts (myeloblasts): 0.8% — within normal limits.
No monoblasts, promonocytes, or aberrant-appearing blasts identified.
No Auer rods identified.
Red blood cell morphology on aspirate tail: normochromic, normocytic. No significant dysplastic red blood cell forms.
Platelet morphology: adequate, predominantly normal-sized.

Comment: The aspirate differential is notable for an M:E ratio of approximately 2.0:1, consistent with recovering myeloid predominance in the early post-engraftment period. Maturational spectrum is intact for all lineages. There is no residual or recurrent AML morphologically.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ANCILLARY STUDIES
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

A. Flow Cytometry — Minimal Residual Disease (MRD) Assessment
Method: 8-color multiparameter flow cytometry, leukemia-associated immunophenotype (LAIP) approach
Panel: CD45, CD34, CD117, CD33, CD13, CD7, MPO, HLA-DR, CD19, CD3, CD56, CD14, CD64, CD11b, CD38, CD123
Total events acquired: 547,000

Result: NEGATIVE for residual / recurrent AML immunophenotype.
No immunophenotypically aberrant blast population is detected above the limit of detection (0.01% of total nucleated cells, or 1 in 10,000 events). The CD34+/CD117+ blast compartment represents <0.1% of total events, within normal limits, with no aberrant antigen co-expression (no CD34+/CD7+, no CD34+/CD56+, no aberrant MPO-bright dim-CD45 population). Maturing myeloid elements show normal antigen acquisition.

Conclusion: No evidence of MRD by 8-color flow cytometry at a sensitivity of 0.01%.

B. Cytogenetics — Conventional Karyotype
Method: G-banding on 48–72-hour unstimulated culture of bone marrow aspirate cells
Metaphases analyzed: 20
Result: 46,XY [20/20 metaphases analyzed]

Interpretation: All 20 metaphases analyzed demonstrate a normal male (46,XY) karyotype, consistent with complete engraftment from the male unrelated donor (recipient karyotype at diagnosis was 46,XX). No cytogenetic abnormalities are detected. The conversion from 46,XX (recipient) to 46,XY (donor) in all metaphases is a definitive indicator of complete donor engraftment at the cytogenetic level.

C. Donor Chimerism by Short Tandem Repeat (STR) Analysis
Method: PCR-based STR profiling of informative loci (AmpFlSTR Identifiler panel), performed on bulk mononuclear cells and lineage-separated fractions
Specimen: Bone marrow aspirate mononuclear cells and peripheral blood fractions

Results:
  Total bone marrow chimerism (unsorted):  98% donor / 2% recipient
  CD3+ (T cell) fraction (peripheral blood): 92% donor / 8% recipient
  CD33+ (myeloid) fraction (peripheral blood): 99% donor / 1% recipient

Interpretation: Engraftment is confirmed with near-complete donor chimerism across all assessed compartments. Myeloid chimerism of 99% donor is consistent with full myeloid engraftment. A small residual recipient signal in the T-cell fraction (8% recipient) is consistent with the known slower kinetics of T-lymphocyte donor conversion following allogeneic PBSCT; this is a common early post-transplant finding and does not indicate graft failure or relapse. Serial chimerism monitoring is recommended.

D. Molecular Studies (PENDING at time of final sign-out — results to be reported as addendum)

  1. FLT3-ITD quantitative PCR (sensitivity 0.001%): PENDING
  2. DNMT3A p.R882H by digital droplet PCR (ddPCR): PENDING
  3. RUNX1 p.S291fs*16 deep sequencing by NGS: PENDING

Note: These molecular MRD assays are performed at a reference laboratory with extended turnaround time of 10–14 business days. Results will be communicated to the treating team as a separate addendum once finalized. The above morphologic and flow cytometric results are not contingent on these molecular findings.

E. Iron Stain (Prussian Blue)
Performed on aspirate smear: Stainable iron present, adequate storage iron. No ring sideroblasts identified (0 ring sideroblasts per 100 erythroid precursors counted). Result: Normal iron stores.

F. Reticulin Stain
Performed on core biopsy: MF-0 to MF-1. Focal mild increase in reticulin fibers without intersections or collagen deposition. Within expected range post-myeloablative conditioning and early engraftment.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PATHOLOGIC DIAGNOSIS / ASSESSMENT
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

BONE MARROW (RIGHT POSTERIOR ILIAC CREST), BIOPSY AND ASPIRATE — DAY +30 POST-ALLOGENEIC HSCT:

1. NORMOCELLULAR BONE MARROW (50–60% CELLULARITY) WITH TRILINEAGE HEMATOPOIESIS AND ENGRAFTMENT.
   - Myeloblasts: <1% (0.8% by 500-cell aspirate differential; morphologic complete remission criteria maintained).
   - No Auer rods. No morphologic evidence of acute myeloid leukemia (AML).

2. DONOR CHIMERISM CONFIRMED:
   - Karyotype 46,XY (donor) in 20/20 metaphases (recipient karyotype was 46,XX).
   - STR-based chimerism: 98% donor total; CD33+ myeloid 99% donor; CD3+ T cell 92% donor.
   - Complete myeloid engraftment confirmed. T-cell chimerism mixed, expected at day +30.

3. MRD NEGATIVE BY 8-COLOR MULTIPARAMETER FLOW CYTOMETRY (sensitivity 0.01%).
   - No immunophenotypically aberrant blast population detected.

4. MOLECULAR MRD STUDIES (FLT3-ITD qPCR, DNMT3A ddPCR, RUNX1 NGS): PENDING — ADDENDUM TO FOLLOW.

COMMENT

This day +30 post-transplant bone marrow biopsy demonstrates successful engraftment of the 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor (MUD) peripheral blood stem cell graft with near-complete donor chimerism, recovering trilineage hematopoiesis, and no morphologic or flow cytometric evidence of residual or recurrent AML. The patient had previously achieved morphologic complete remission with MRD negativity by flow cytometry following a single cycle of 7+3 induction chemotherapy plus midostaurin, and this biopsy confirms sustained remission in the post-transplant engraftment period.

The residual recipient T-cell signal (8%) is a commonly observed phenomenon in the early post-transplant period following peripheral blood stem cell transplant, reflecting the slower clearance and replacement of host-derived T lymphocytes. This is not considered evidence of mixed chimerism of clinical concern at this time point and should be monitored at subsequent assessments.

Molecular MRD results (FLT3-ITD, DNMT3A R882H, RUNX1 frameshift) are pending and will provide additional sensitivity for residual disease detection beyond what is achievable by morphology and flow cytometry. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Chen is a 48-year-old woman with newly diagnosed FLT3-ITD-positive AML (high allelic ratio, NPM1-unmutated, RUNX1-mutated, DNMT3A-mutated), ELN 2022 Adverse risk, who achieved a first complete remission with MRD negativity after a single cycle of 7+3 induction with midostaurin. She is now presenting in excellent condition for pre-transplant evaluation. All organ function assessments are satisfactory for myeloablative allogeneic transplant. A 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated male donor has been identified.

1. TRANSPLANT INDICATION AND STRATEGY:
Allogeneic SCT in first CR is strongly indicated and supported by current ELN 2022 guidelines and published data for patients with adverse-risk AML. The combination of FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio in an NPM1-unmutated setting, compounded by the presence of RUNX1 frameshift and DNMT3A R882H co-mutations, confers a very high risk of relapse with chemotherapy alone, estimated at >70-80% without consolidative transplant. The achievement of MRD negativity prior to transplant is an excellent prognostic feature and should be carried into the transplant. Allogeneic transplant offers both ablation of residual disease and the graft-versus-leukemia (GVL) effect, which is especially important in this setting.

Donor: 10/10 HLA-matched (allele-level) unrelated male donor, age 32, CMV seropositive. Donor workup is in progress. Peripheral blood stem cells will be used as the graft source (standard for MUD transplants in our program to ensure robust engraftment).

2. CONDITIONING REGIMEN:
Myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4) is planned. Fludarabine 40 mg/m2 IV daily on days -6 through -3, combined with busulfan 3.2 mg/kg IV daily on days -6 through -3, pharmacokinetically dosed targeting AUC of 4000-5000 umol*min/L/day. Anti-seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam will be administered for the duration of busulfan administration.

Rationale for myeloablative (rather than reduced-intensity) conditioning: patient age (48 years), ECOG PS 0-1, preserved organ function, and adverse-risk disease all favor myeloablative conditioning to maximize disease eradication.

3. GVHD PROPHYLAXIS:
Tacrolimus (target trough 5-15 ng/mL) plus short-course MTX (15 mg/m2 on day +1; 10 mg/m2 on days +3, +6, +11). This is our institutional standard for MUD transplants. Post-transplant CTX was discussed but not selected given 10/10 HLA match.

4. INFECTIOUS PROPHYLAXIS:
- CMV: Letermovir 480 mg daily starting day +1 for CMV prophylaxis (both donor and recipient are CMV seropositive — high-risk pair)
- Antifungal: micafungin during neutropenic phase; transition to azole-based prophylaxis post-engraftment per protocol
- Bacterial: fluoroquinolone prophylaxis during neutropenic phase per institutional protocol
- PJP: TMP-SMX to be held during conditioning and restarted post-engraftment
- HSV/VZV: acyclovir to be continued
- PneumococcalVaccination and other post-transplant vaccinations: deferred per protocol timeline

5. FLT3 INHIBITOR MAINTENANCE POST-TRANSPLANT:
Post-transplant FLT3 inhibitor maintenance therapy will be discussed and considered after day +60 assessment pending engraftment confirmation and tolerance. Current evidence supports consideration of this approach in FLT3-ITD-positive patients post-alloHSCT, though it is not part of the current plan until engraftment and early recovery are confirmed.

6. BONE MARROW ASSESSMENT SCHEDULE:
Post-transplant bone marrow biopsy and aspirate with chimerism, MRD, and molecular studies planned at day +30 and day +100 at minimum, with additional assessments per protocol or clinical indication.

7. FERTILITY COUNSELING:
Myeloablative conditioning with busulfan is expected to cause permanent ovarian failure and infertility. Ms. Chen was counseled regarding this expected late effect. She was informed that fertility preservation options (oocyte or embryo cryopreservation) were available prior to induction but were not pursued given the urgency of treatment at diagnosis. She verbalized understanding of the expected loss of fertility. Hormone replacement therapy will be discussed post-transplant with gynecology/endocrinology as appropriate.

8. INFORMED CONSENT:
Extensive informed consent discussion conducted today in the presence of her husband. Topics covered included: goals of transplant, conditioning regimen and expected toxicities (mucositis, cytopenias, infection risk, hepatic sinusoidal obstruction syndrome/VOD — low risk with FluBu4 but discussed), acute GVHD, chronic GVHD, graft failure, secondary malignancy risk, late effects of busulfan (pulmonary, cataracts, gonadal failure), and transplant-related mortality (estimated at 10-15% for a 10/10 MUD in this age group with this conditioning). The patient and her husband asked thoughtful questions and demonstrated understanding of the risks and benefits. Written consent obtained.

9. LOGISTICS:
- Admission for conditioning anticipated to begin in approximately 1-2 weeks pending donor readiness and final donor evaluation
- Central venous catheter (tunneled/Hickman) placement will be arranged prior to admission
- Dental clearance: complete
- Psychosocial clearance: complete
- Caregiver: husband confirmed and educated
- Housing: patient lives locally; discharge planning within close proximity of transplant center confirmed

Patient is an excellent transplant candidate. She is well-prepared, in excellent functional status, in first morphologic and molecular CR, and has an optimal 10/10 MUD identified. Proceed as planned.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Margaret Chen is a 48-year-old female presenting for her first scheduled outpatient transplant clinic visit following discharge from the inpatient bone marrow transplant unit. She is now approximately 45 days post matched unrelated donor (MUD) allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (alloHSCT) for adverse-risk acute myeloid leukemia (AML). She was discharged home on day +28 and this is her first formal post-discharge assessment in our outpatient clinic.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen is a 48-year-old female biology teacher with a history of hypothyroidism who was diagnosed approximately three and a half months ago with de novo AML, NOS, with an adverse-risk molecular profile. At diagnosis, she presented with a white count of 87,400/uL with 40% circulating blasts, hemoglobin of 6.8 g/dL, and platelets of 32,000/uL, along with prominent gingival hyperplasia and bleeding. Bone marrow biopsy at diagnosis demonstrated 72% myeloblasts in a hypercellular marrow (95% cellularity). Immunophenotype was CD34+/CD117+/CD33+/CD13+/MPO+/HLA-DR+. Cytogenetics revealed a normal karyotype (46,XX). Comprehensive myeloid NGS panel showed a high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD (33 bp insertion, VAF 40.2%, allelic ratio 0.68) along with co-mutations in DNMT3A (p.R882H, VAF 48.1%), RUNX1 (p.S291fs*16, VAF 43.7%), and a subclonal TET2 nonsense mutation. The combination of high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD with NPM1-unmutated disease and RUNX1 frameshift placed this firmly in the ELN 2022 adverse-risk category.

She was treated with 7+3 induction chemotherapy (cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day continuous infusion days 1-7 plus daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 on days 1-3) with midostaurin 50 mg PO twice daily on days 8-21 per the RATIFY protocol. Her induction course was complicated by febrile neutropenia with bilateral lower lobe pneumonia requiring empiric cefepime and micafungin, and by grade 2 mucositis. Grade 2 nausea/vomiting attributed to midostaurin was managed with prochlorperazine. Day 28 bone marrow biopsy confirmed morphologic complete remission with less than 5% blasts (3%) and MRD negativity by 8-color flow cytometry. FLT3-ITD was undetectable by PCR.

Given her adverse-risk disease profile, she proceeded directly to allogeneic SCT without additional consolidation chemotherapy. Her one full sibling was a 7/10 HLA match and was not suitable as donor. Unrelated donor search identified a 10/10 HLA-matched (A, B, C, DRB1, DQB1) unrelated male donor, age 32, CMV seropositive — a well-matched donor. Pre-transplant evaluation confirmed ECOG PS 0-1, adequate cardiac function (LVEF 60%), normal pulmonary function (FEV1 88% predicted, DLCO 82%), and normal organ function.

She received myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4), pharmacokinetically dosed targeting AUC 4000-5000 umol*min/L per day, with levetiracetam seizure prophylaxis during busulfan administration. Day 0 infusion of peripheral blood stem cells from the MUD occurred without incident; CD34+ cell dose was 5.2 x 10^6/kg. GVHD prophylaxis was tacrolimus (continuous IV infusion, trough target 5-15 ng/mL, transitioned to oral when tolerating PO) plus methotrexate 15 mg/m2 on day +1 and 10 mg/m2 on days +3, +6, and +11. CMV prophylaxis with letermovir 480 mg daily was started on day +1. Antifungal coverage with micafungin was continued throughout the aplastic phase. TMP-SMX was held during conditioning and the peri-engraftment period.

The post-transplant inpatient course was notable for grade 3 mucositis developing around day +8, which required TPN and IV morphine PCA for pain control, along with grade 2 nausea/vomiting and grade 1 diarrhea. Mucositis gradually improved and she was tolerating oral intake by day +18. A supratherapeutic tacrolimus level (25 ng/mL on day +10) required dose reduction and was associated with transient creatinine elevation to 1.4 mg/dL from a baseline of 0.8 mg/dL; renal function recovered to creatinine 1.0 mg/dL by day +20 with dose adjustment. Engraftment occurred with ANC recovery by day +18.

Day +30 bone marrow biopsy (see prior pathology report) confirmed engraftment: normocellular marrow (50-60% cellularity), less than 1% blasts, MRD negative by flow cytometry, and 46,XY karyotype in all 20 metaphases analyzed — confirming full donor chimerism. STR-based chimerism analysis showed 98% donor total chimerism (99% myeloid, 92% T cell). She was discharged on day +28 with a stable clinical status.

She presents today 17 days post-discharge for her day +45 follow-up. Overall, she reports feeling gradually better. Energy levels remain moderately reduced compared to her pre-illness baseline but are improved compared to discharge. She is able to perform all activities of daily living. She is spending most of her time at home. Her husband has been providing caregiver support.

She denies fever, chills, or rigors. She denies significant cough, shortness of breath, or chest pain. Appetite is improving; she is tolerating a soft to regular diet. She reports 2-3 loose stools per day, which she describes as her current baseline — no blood, no mucus, no significant cramping. She has not noticed any new skin rashes. She reports some residual oral dryness.

She is taking all her medications as prescribed. She notes occasional mild nausea, manageable without the need for additional antiemetics. She denies new rashes, visual changes, joint pain, or edema.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Margaret Chen is a 48-year-old female biology teacher with a history of de novo FLT3-ITD–positive AML (ELN 2022 Adverse risk) who presents for her day +100 post-transplant follow-up visit. For context, she was diagnosed approximately 100 days ago with AML presenting with a WBC of 87,400/uL, gingival hyperplasia, and bone marrow biopsy confirming 72% myeloblasts with Auer rods. Molecular workup demonstrated a high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD (AR 0.68), co-occurring DNMT3A R882H, RUNX1 frameshift, and TET2 nonsense mutations. She was treated with 7+3 induction (cytarabine/daunorubicin) plus midostaurin per RATIFY protocol, achieving morphologic complete remission with MRD negativity after a single induction cycle. Given her adverse-risk disease and availability of a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor (MUD), she was taken to allogeneic HSCT without intervening consolidation. Conditioning was FluBu4 (myeloablative, pharmacokinetically dosed) with tacrolimus/methotrexate GVHD prophylaxis. Day 0 peripheral blood stem cell infusion delivered a CD34+ cell dose of 5.2 x 10^6/kg from a 32-year-old CMV-seropositive male donor.

Her post-transplant course was notable for grade 3 mucositis with TPN requirement through approximately day +18, and transiently elevated tacrolimus levels requiring dose reduction (creatinine peaked at 1.4 mg/dL, subsequently returned to baseline). She achieved engraftment and was discharged on day +28. At her day +45 visit she was doing well with no GVHD and full count recovery.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Margaret Chen is a 48-year-old woman with AML (FLT3-ITD positive, ELN Adverse risk) in complete morphologic and molecular remission following 7+3/midostaurin induction and 10/10 MUD allogeneic HSCT with FluBu4 myeloablative conditioning. She presents at day +100 with new skin findings and persistent mild GI symptoms.

1. NEW SKIN CHANGES — SUSPECTED CHRONIC GRAFT-VERSUS-HOST DISEASE (cGVHD), SKIN:
The new erythematous, slightly violaceous lichenoid papules on bilateral forearms and dorsal hands, involving approximately 10% BSA, are highly concerning for cutaneous chronic GVHD. The timing at day +100, the morphology (lichenoid vs. lichen planus–like papules with violaceous hue), and the distribution are all characteristic. This is distinct from acute GVHD, which typically presents earlier and with a maculopapular eruption; however, I note that the distinction between late acute and early chronic GVHD at the day +100 timeframe can be nuanced. The clinical picture here most closely fits an early cGVHD pattern, and this will be confirmed with biopsy.
 - Skin punch biopsy to be performed today at the right forearm papule and sent to dermatopathology for evaluation. Will look for basal vacuolization, dyskeratotic keratinocytes, and lichenoid interface infiltrate, which would confirm cGVHD.
 - Dermatology consultation requested for co-management of skin GVHD.
 - Initiate topical corticosteroid therapy empirically given high clinical suspicion: clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment to affected areas on bilateral forearms and dorsal hands twice daily. Counsel patient to use sparingly and avoid application to thin skin areas such as the face and flexural folds.
 - Systemic immunosuppression escalation deferred pending biopsy confirmation and assessment of overall GVHD severity. Tacrolimus will continue at current dose (trough 10.8 ng/mL, therapeutic).
 - Do NOT taper tacrolimus at this time — taper plan deferred until cGVHD is better characterized and controlled.
 - Classify under NIH cGVHD criteria: if skin involvement confirmed as lichenoid, this would correspond to NIH skin score 2 (10–18% BSA with lichenoid features). Overall NIH global severity will be reassessed once the full picture is clear.

2. GI SYMPTOMS — POSSIBLE GI cGVHD vs. POST-TRANSPLANT DYSMOTILITY:
Loose stools 1–2 per day with mild cramping could reflect early GI chronic GVHD involving the lower GI tract, but other etiologies must also be considered, including post-transplant dysmotility, ongoing recovery from mucositis-related mucosal injury, and CMV colitis (though CMV PCR is undetectable on letermovir). The lack of blood, mucus, nocturnal stools, or significant weight loss is somewhat reassuring. C. difficile toxin PCR is added to today's workup given that the patient is on multiple antibiotics (TMP-SMX, acyclovir, fluconazole).
 - Stool C. difficile PCR ordered today.
 - If C. difficile negative and symptoms persist or worsen, will consider lower GI endoscopy to evaluate for GI cGVHD.
 - Empirically add oral budesonide 3 mg PO three times daily, given suspicion for GI cGVHD contributing to symptoms. Budesonide is preferred for its topical GI activity with limited systemic absorption, allowing treatment without substantial additional immunosuppression.
 - Dietary counseling: low-residue, low-lactose diet recommended; referral to transplant dietitian.
 - Encourage adequate hydration. Monitor weight at each visit.

3. MILDLY ELEVATED LIVER ENZYMES (ALT 48, AST 38):
Mild transaminase elevation is noted today. The differential in this clinical context includes hepatic cGVHD (NIH liver score 1 if attributable to cGVHD, as ALT is 1–3x ULN), tacrolimus-related hepatotoxicity, fluconazole effect, or recovering viral hepatitis (unlikely given negative EBV PCR). Total bilirubin is normal, alkaline phosphatase is normal, and albumin is preserved, making significant hepatic GVHD less likely at this juncture.
 - Repeat LFTs in 2 weeks.
 - If ALT or AST rises above 3x ULN or bilirubin becomes elevated, liver GVHD workup will be expanded and gastroenterology/hepatology will be consulted.
 - Continue to monitor tacrolimus levels closely.

4. CANCER STATUS — AML, COMPLETE REMISSION:
Margaret remains in morphologic and molecular complete remission. No clinical features are concerning for relapse today. CBC is reassuring with no blasts identified on differential. Tacrolimus at therapeutic levels provides ongoing graft-versus-leukemia support. Bone marrow biopsy is not indicated at this time; will plan for routine marrow assessment at approximately day +180 per protocol.
 - Continue to monitor CBC with differential at each visit.
 - Peripheral blood chimerism to be checked at next visit (day +120) to confirm ongoing full donor chimerism.

5. INFECTIOUS PROPHYLAXIS — CONTINUE:
No active infections identified. CMV PCR undetectable on letermovir. EBV undetectable. Fungal markers negative.
 - Continue letermovir 480 mg daily through at least day +100 (now transitioning to day +180 per institutional protocol given ongoing immunosuppression/cGVHD).
 - Continue acyclovir, fluconazole, TMP-SMX at current doses.
 - Counsel patient to continue strict infection precautions (hand hygiene, avoidance of sick contacts, food safety). She remains immunocompromised at day +100 with tacrolimus on board.

6. TACROLIMUS MONITORING AND ELECTROLYTES:
Tacrolimus trough is therapeutic at 10.8 ng/mL. Magnesium is low-normal at 1.7 mEq/L, consistent with tacrolimus-induced renal magnesium wasting.
 - Add magnesium oxide 400 mg PO BID supplementation.
 - Recheck magnesium in 2 weeks.
 - Continue tacrolimus dose monitoring; will not taper at this visit given new cGVHD diagnosis.

7. HYPOTHYROIDISM:
TSH within normal range on current levothyroxine dose. No adjustments needed. Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily.

8. GENERAL SUPPORTIVE CARE AND QUALITY OF LIFE:
 - Patient has mild xerostomia; continue saliva substitute and recommend good oral hygiene. Refer to transplant dentistry for routine dental evaluation.
 - Encourage graduated physical activity as tolerated.
 - Psychosocial support offered; patient and husband report feeling cautiously optimistic but anxious about the new rash. Reassured that cGVHD, if confirmed, is a treatable condition and that mild cGVHD is actually associated with a lower risk of disease relapse.
 - Return to work (part-time teaching) can be considered in a limited capacity once cGVHD is under control and immunosuppression is stable, though patient should avoid settings with high exposure to respiratory illnesses.

9. FOLLOW-UP:
 - Return to clinic in 2 weeks to review biopsy result, reassess skin GVHD response to topical clobetasol, reassess GI symptoms on budesonide, and review C. difficile result and LFTs.
 - At that 2-week visit, formal NIH cGVHD global severity scoring will be completed with input from dermatology, and a GVHD management plan will be finalized.
 - Patient provided with emergency contact information and instructed to call if she develops fever >38°C, worsening diarrhea (more than 4 stools/day), blood in stools, jaundice, difficulty breathing, or significant worsening of skin rash.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION AND DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS

CORE BIOPSY (Specimen A):
The core biopsy is markedly hypercellular, with estimated cellularity of 90-95% (virtually entirely replaced by hematopoietic cells; fat spaces nearly absent). The marrow space is diffusely infiltrated by a monotonous population of large, immature cells with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios, vesicular nuclei with one to three prominent nucleoli, and scant to moderate amounts of pale basophilic cytoplasm. Mitotic figures are readily identified. Scattered Auer rod-containing cells are appreciated on the touch preparations and confirmed on aspirate smears (see below). Residual erythropoiesis and thrombopoiesis are markedly diminished. Scattered mature neutrophils are present but represent a minor fraction of the total cellularity. The bony trabeculae show no osteoblastic or osteoclastic activity and no fibrosis beyond trace reticulin (MF-0 by European Consensus grading). Sinusoidal architecture is effaced by the neoplastic infiltrate. No granulomas, necrosis, or metastatic carcinoma identified.

ASPIRATE SMEARS AND CLOT SECTION (Specimen B):
The aspirate smears are markedly hypercellular with abundant spicules. The 500-cell differential reveals:
  - Myeloblasts: 72%
  - Promyelocytes: 3%
  - Myelocytes/metamyelocytes: 2%
  - Segmented neutrophils and bands: 5%
  - Monocytes: 2%
  - Erythroid precursors: 10%
  - Lymphocytes: 5%
  - Plasma cells: 1%

The blast population consists of large cells with round to slightly irregular nuclei, finely dispersed (open) chromatin, prominent nucleoli (one to three per cell), and moderate amounts of pale to lightly basophilic cytoplasm. Azurophilic granules are present in the cytoplasm of a subset of blasts. Auer rods — elongated, needle-like, magenta-staining cytoplasmic inclusions — are identified in approximately 8-12% of blasts on optimal smear preparation; no faggot cells (bundles of Auer rods) are observed. These findings are pathognomonic of myeloid differentiation and exclude lymphoblastic leukemia. The clot section confirms the findings from the core biopsy with sheets of immature blasts. Erythroid and megakaryocytic elements are markedly reduced. Megakaryocytes present are morphologically normal without dysplastic features.

PERIPHERAL BLOOD SMEAR (Specimen C):
The peripheral smear confirms leukocytosis with a marked left shift. Circulating blasts account for approximately 38-42% of the differential count. The blasts morphologically resemble those described in the aspirate, with round nuclei, open chromatin, prominent nucleoli, and moderate basophilic cytoplasm; Auer rods are identified in a subset of circulating blasts. Significant neutropenia with relative predominance of immature granulocytic forms (blasts, occasional promyelocytes). Anemia with normochromic, normocytic red blood cells and no dysplastic erythroid changes (no megaloblastic changes, no ring sideroblasts anticipated). Thrombocytopenia confirmed. COMMENT
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
The morphologic, cytochemical, and immunophenotypic findings are diagnostic of acute myeloid leukemia. The blast percentage of 72% significantly exceeds the WHO threshold of ≥20% for AML diagnosis. The presence of Auer rods is pathognomonic for myeloid differentiation and effectively excludes ALL/LBL. The blast immunophenotype — CD34+, CD117+, CD33+, CD13+, MPO+ (flow and histochemistry), HLA-DR+, with aberrant dim CD7 — is entirely consistent with AML with no morphologic or immunophenotypic features to suggest a specific recurrent genetic entity (pending molecular data). The absence of t(8;21), inv(16), and t(15;17) by FISH, and the normal karyotype by G-banding, establish this as cytogenetically normal AML (NK-AML). No significant dysplastic changes are appreciated in the residual non-blast hematopoietic elements; combined with the de novo clinical presentation (no prior MDS or antecedent hematologic disorder documented), the findings do not support a classification of AML with myelodysplasia-related changes on morphologic grounds alone.

Final ELN 2022 risk stratification is deferred pending molecular studies, particularly FLT3-ITD allelic ratio, NPM1, CEBPA, and comprehensive NGS panel results. These results will be critical for determining the prognostic category and guiding decisions regarding allogeneic stem cell transplantation in first remission.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Sections of skin demonstrate an epidermis with focal irregular acanthosis and mild hyperkeratosis without parakeratosis. The most striking histologic finding is a band-like to focally interface pattern of lymphocytic infiltrate situated along the dermal-epidermal junction (DEJ). The lymphocytes are predominantly small and mature appearing, without cytologic atypia. The infiltrate extends into the superficial papillary dermis in areas.

Interface changes are prominent and include:
- Basal layer vacuolization (vacuolar degeneration/liquefactive degeneration of basal keratinocytes), diffuse and multifocal throughout the biopsy
- Scattered individually necrotic (dyskeratotic) keratinocytes within the basal layer and lower spinous layer; these cells display deeply eosinophilic, pyknotic cytoplasm with condensed nuclear debris (Civatte bodies / colloid bodies)
- Occasional satellite cell necrosis: individual dyskeratotic keratinocytes intimately associated with lymphocytes, a finding highly characteristic of graft-versus-host disease
- Focal pigment incontinence: melanin-laden macrophages within the superficial papillary dermis, consistent with established basal layer injury

The epidermal rete ridge pattern shows mild effacement/flattening in areas. No significant spongiosis. No epidermal vesiculation or full-thickness necrosis. No neutrophilic infiltrate within the epidermis.

The superficial dermis contains a moderately dense, predominantly perivascular and interstitial lymphocytic infiltrate composed of small mature lymphocytes. No eosinophils are identified. No plasma cells. No neutrophils. Rare mast cells present in the papillary dermis.

Adnexal structures: eccrine sweat glands are present and show focal peri-eccrine lymphocytic infiltrate without significant glandular destruction. Hair follicles present but limited on this biopsy; no follicular dropout identified. Sebaceous glands appear preserved.

The deep dermis demonstrates mild fibroplasia with increased collagen deposition, consistent with early fibrotic change. No sclerosis or hyalinization is identified, which would be more consistent with a sclerotic/morpheaform type of cGVHD.

Subcutaneous tissue: not well represented in this specimen.

No granulomas. No fungal organisms, viral inclusions, or parasites are identified on routine H&E sections. No features to suggest a primary infectious etiology.

No atypical lymphoid cells are identified; specifically, no large cells, no Reed-Sternberg-like cells, no epidermotropic large atypical cells. The lymphocytic infiltrate does not form nodular aggregates or Pautrier microabscesses.

DIAGNOSIS:

A. Skin, right forearm, punch biopsy:
   LICHENOID (INTERFACE) DERMATITIS CONSISTENT WITH CHRONIC GRAFT-VERSUS-HOST DISEASE, LICHENOID TYPE

   - Interface dermatitis with prominent basal layer vacuolization
   - Scattered dyskeratotic keratinocytes (Civatte bodies) with satellite cell necrosis
   - Band-like lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermal-epidermal junction
   - Mild dermal fibroplasia
   - No evidence of malignancy
   - No morphologic evidence of infection on H&E sections (special stains pending)
   - Immunohistochemistry pending (see addendum)

   NIH Histologic Grade (Skin GVHD): Grade 2
   (Basal vacuolization with dyskeratosis and band-like infiltrate; no full-thickness epidermal involvement or fibrosis)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
COMMENT:

The histomorphologic findings in this 4 mm punch biopsy of the right forearm are characteristic of chronic graft-versus-host disease, lichenoid subtype. The combination of interface change with basal vacuolization, dyskeratotic (apoptotic) keratinocytes, satellite cell necrosis, and a band-like lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermal-epidermal junction without significant eosinophilia is the prototypical pattern of GVHD and is highly distinguishable from a simple drug reaction (which typically shows eosinophils and lacks the degree of satellite cell necrosis) or other lichenoid dermatoses such as lichen planus (which would typically show a more compact, "saw-tooth" rete ridge pattern and the presence of Civatte bodies without the transplant context).

The clinical context is strongly supportive: the patient is approximately 100 days post allogeneic stem cell transplantation from a 10/10 MUD donor, presenting with erythematous papules on the forearms and dorsal hands in a distribution characteristic of lichenoid cGVHD.

The NIH consensus criteria for skin GVHD histologic grading are applied as follows:
  - Grade 1: Vacuolization of basal cells only
  - Grade 2: Vacuolization with dyskeratosis (as seen here)
  - Grade 3: Subepidermal cleft formation
  - Grade 4: Complete epidermal separation

These findings correlate with Grade 2 histologic involvement, consistent with the clinical assessment of mild-to-moderate chronic GVHD per NIH global scoring criteria.

The early dermal fibroplasia is noted and suggests chronicity of the process; sclerotic cGVHD (lichen sclerosus-like pattern with dermal hyalinization and sclerosis) is not present at this time but should be monitored on subsequent biopsies.

Immunohistochemistry results will be issued as an addendum when finalized. PAS and GMS stain results will also be reported at that time; however, the absence of organisms on H&E sections and the overall clinical context make infectious dermatitis unlikely.

Correlation with clinical presentation and NIH global scoring of all organ involvement is recommended for determination of overall cGVHD severity and management decisions.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Chen is a 48-year-old high school biology teacher with no prior hematologic history who was diagnosed with de novo acute myeloid leukemia (AML), NOS, at age 48. Her presenting features included a leukocyte count of approximately 87,400/uL with ~40% circulating blasts, hemoglobin of 6.8 g/dL, platelets of 32,000/uL, and gingival hyperplasia with spontaneous bleeding. Bone marrow biopsy at diagnosis demonstrated 72% myeloblasts in a hypercellular marrow (95% cellularity), with Auer rods present and immunophenotype consistent with AML (CD34+, CD117+, CD33+, CD13+, MPO+). Molecular workup was notable for FLT3-ITD with a high allelic ratio of 0.68, co-occurring with DNMT3A p.R882H and RUNX1 frameshift mutations, placing her in the ELN 2022 Adverse risk category. Cytogenetics showed a normal female karyotype. NPM1, IDH1/IDH2, and TP53 were all wild-type.

She received 7+3 induction chemotherapy (cytarabine 100 mg/m2 continuous infusion days 1-7 and daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 days 1-3) with the addition of midostaurin 50 mg BID on days 8-21 per the RATIFY protocol. Her induction course was complicated by febrile neutropenia with bilateral lower lobe pulmonary infiltrates (treated with cefepime and micafungin for 14 days), grade 2 mucositis, and grade 2 nausea/vomiting attributed to midostaurin. Day +28 bone marrow biopsy confirmed morphologic complete remission with MRD negativity by 8-color flow cytometry. Given her adverse-risk disease, she proceeded expeditiously to allogeneic stem cell transplant without consolidation chemotherapy.

A 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated male donor (age 32, CMV seropositive) was identified. Pre-transplant evaluation confirmed adequate cardiac (LVEF 60%), pulmonary (FEV1 88%, DLCO 82%), and renal function. PET-CT prior to transplant demonstrated complete metabolic response with normalization of splenomegaly. She received myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4, pharmacokinetically dosed) with GVHD prophylaxis consisting of tacrolimus and short-course methotrexate. Day 0 peripheral blood stem cell infusion proceeded without incident (CD34+ cell dose 5.2 x 10^6/kg). Post-transplant course was complicated by grade 3 mucositis (requiring TPN and IV morphine PCA) and a transient tacrolimus toxicity episode with creatinine rising to 1.4 mg/dL, both of which resolved. Engraftment was confirmed at day +30 with 98% donor chimerism and MRD negativity.

At her day +100 clinic visit, she developed mild-to-moderate chronic GVHD with lichenoid skin changes covering approximately 10-15% BSA on bilateral forearms and dorsal hands, along with loose stools 2-3 times daily with mild cramping. Skin punch biopsy confirmed lichenoid cGVHD (NIH grade 2). Overall NIH global cGVHD severity was scored as mild. She was started on topical clobetasol ointment for her skin and oral budesonide 3 mg TID for GI symptoms, with tacrolimus taper deferred.

Today, at day +180, she returns for surveillance. She reports significant improvement in her skin GVHD -- the erythematous papules on her forearms and hands have largely resolved with ongoing topical clobetasol. She is down to applying clobetasol to small residual areas only. Her stools have improved markedly on budesonide, now 1-2 loosely formed stools per day with minimal cramping. She denies any oral mucosal pain though notes ongoing mild dryness of the mouth requiring frequent sips of water and use of a saliva substitute spray. She has no fever, no chills, no night sweats. No new skin lesions. No nausea or vomiting. No bleeding or bruising. She denies bone pain, joint pain, or muscle cramping. She reports gradual improvement in her stamina and energy levels and has begun returning to light gardening activities at home. She is not yet back to teaching but is optimistic about returning in the coming months.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Chen is a 49-year-old female high school biology teacher with a history of de novo AML (FLT3-ITD positive with high allelic ratio of 0.68, along with co-mutations in DNMT3A, RUNX1, and TET2 — ELN 2022 Adverse risk), who presented approximately one year ago with progressive fatigue, gingival bleeding, easy bruising, and a 12-pound unintentional weight loss. At diagnosis, her WBC was 87,400/uL with circulating blasts at approximately 40%, hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL, and platelets 32,000/uL. Bone marrow biopsy at that time demonstrated a hypercellular marrow (95% cellularity) with 72% myeloblasts and Auer rods on smear.

She received standard 7+3 induction (cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day continuous infusion days 1-7 plus daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 days 1-3) in combination with midostaurin 50 mg PO BID on days 8-21 per the RATIFY protocol. Her induction course was complicated by febrile neutropenia with bilateral lower lobe pulmonary consolidations (treated with cefepime and micafungin over a 14-day course), grade 2 mucositis, and midostaurin-associated nausea. She achieved a morphologic complete remission (CR) with MRD negativity by 8-color flow cytometry on day 28 bone marrow biopsy. Given her adverse-risk disease — FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio in a NPM1-unmutated, normal karyotype setting, compounded by RUNX1 frameshift and DNMT3A mutations — the decision was made to proceed directly to allogeneic SCT without consolidation chemotherapy.

Her sibling was a 7/10 HLA match (not suitable), and a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated male donor (age 32, CMV seropositive) was identified through the unrelated donor registry. Pre-transplant restaging PET-CT confirmed complete metabolic remission with no FDG-avid disease. She underwent myeloablative FluBu4 conditioning (fludarabine 40 mg/m2/day and pharmacokinetically dosed busulfan 3.2 mg/kg/day, days -6 through -3) with levetiracetam seizure prophylaxis, and received peripheral blood stem cells on Day 0. GVHD prophylaxis was tacrolimus/MTX, with letermovir CMV prophylaxis starting Day +1.

The early post-transplant period was complicated by grade 3 mucositis requiring TPN and IV morphine PCA (resolving by Day +18), transiently supratherapeutic tacrolimus levels with mild acute kidney injury (creatinine peak 1.4 mg/dL, returned to baseline), and no bacteremia or documented invasive fungal infection. Engraftment was confirmed on Day +30 bone marrow biopsy with 98% total donor chimerism, 99% CD33+ myeloid chimerism, and MRD negativity.

She was discharged on Day +28. At the Day +100 visit she developed clinical chronic GVHD: lichenoid erythematous papules on bilateral forearms and dorsal hands (~10-15% BSA), confirmed histologically on skin punch biopsy showing lichenoid tissue reaction with basal layer vacuolization and dyskeratotic keratinocytes (NIH grade 2 skin GVHD). Mild GI involvement with 2-3 loose stools per day and mild cramping was also noted (NIH GI score 1). Overall NIH global cGVHD severity was mild. She was managed with topical clobetasol to skin lesions, oral budesonide 3 mg TID for GI symptoms, and continuation of tacrolimus without escalation to systemic steroids. By Day +180, skin GVHD had markedly improved to <5% BSA involvement, and GI symptoms had partially improved.

At her 6-month bone marrow biopsy (Day +180), she maintained morphologic and molecular CR with 99.5% total donor chimerism, 100% CD33+ myeloid chimerism, and MRD negativity. Today's visit marks her 1-year post-transplant comprehensive evaluation (Day +365).

Since the Day +180 visit, she reports continued gradual improvement in all parameters. Her GI cGVHD fully resolved and budesonide was successfully tapered and discontinued at month 9 post-transplant. Tacrolimus was successfully tapered and discontinued at month 11 (approximately 6 weeks ago) without any GVHD flare. She reports her energy levels have returned substantially to baseline. She has returned to part-time teaching and describes her quality of life as good, noting some residual mild fatigue and occasional xerostomia but otherwise tolerating daily activities well. She denies any recurrence of skin rash, diarrhea, or oral mucosal ulceration. She reports normal appetite and stable weight. No fevers, chills, night sweats, or new lymphadenopathy. No dyspnea, cough, or pleuritic pain. No neurological complaints. Thyroid function has been stable on levothyroxine.

Today's bone marrow biopsy and aspirate were performed and results are reviewed below.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. ACUTE MYELOID LEUKEMIA, FLT3-ITD HIGH ALLELIC RATIO, ADVERSE ELN RISK -- IN SUSTAINED COMPLETE MOLECULAR REMISSION AT 1 YEAR POST-ALLO SCT

This is an excellent result. Ms. Chen had adverse-risk AML with FLT3-ITD (high allelic ratio 0.68), DNMT3A R882H, RUNX1 frameshift, and TET2 nonsense mutations -- a genomic profile associated with significant relapse risk even after intensive therapy. She achieved MRD-negative CR after a single induction cycle of 7+3 plus midostaurin, and underwent a successful 10/10 MUD alloHSCT with FluBu4 myeloablative conditioning. She now presents at the 1-year anniversary with full donor chimerism (99.8% total, 100% CD33+), MRD negativity by flow cytometry and sensitive molecular assays (qPCR for FLT3-ITD, ddPCR for DNMT3A), and clean bone marrow morphology.

These findings are highly reassuring. The graft-versus-leukemia (GvL) effect conferred by her mild cGVHD likely contributes to disease control. Although the first two years post-transplant represent the highest risk period for relapse in FLT3-ITD AML, her current molecular remission status is very encouraging.

- Continue surveillance every 3 months with CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, and peripheral blood chimerism
- Bone marrow biopsy to be considered at 18 months post-transplant, or sooner if any concerning peripheral blood findings emerge (unexplained cytopenia, loss of chimerism, or reappearance of FLT3-ITD by peripheral blood PCR)
- Patient educated regarding warning signs of relapse: new-onset fatigue, bruising, bleeding, lymphadenopathy, gingival swelling, or unexplained cytopenias -- return to care urgently if these occur
- Post-transplant maintenance therapy with FLT3 inhibitor (e.g., Nexavar or gilteritinib) was considered but not pursued given: (1) MRD-negative status at all post-transplant time points, (2) sustained full donor chimerism, (3) ongoing mild cGVHD providing GvL effect, and (4) absence of high-level evidence for maintenance in this specific scenario at this center's current practice. Will continue monitoring closely; discussion of maintenance can be revisited if any molecular signal emerges.

Ms. Chen's cGVHD has resolved impressively. CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Chen is a 49-year-old female with a history of adverse-risk AML (FLT3-ITD, high allelic ratio) who presents for her 18-month post-allogeneic stem cell transplant surveillance visit. She has no new complaints. She reports feeling well overall and has returned to her normal activities.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Chen is well known to our transplant program. By way of brief history, she was diagnosed approximately 18 months ago with de novo AML at age 48, presenting with symptomatic leukocytosis (WBC 87,400/uL), severe anemia (hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL), thrombocytopenia (platelets 32,000/uL), gingival hyperplasia with spontaneous bleeding, and circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy at diagnosis confirmed hypercellular marrow with 72% myeloblasts, Auer rods, and immunophenotype consistent with AML, NOS. NGS demonstrated FLT3-ITD with allelic ratio 0.68 (high), DNMT3A R882H, RUNX1 S291fs frameshift, TET2 Q916* (subclonal), and WT1 R462Q -- a heavily mutated, ELN Adverse-risk profile.

She underwent induction chemotherapy with standard 7+3 (cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day x 7 days continuous infusion, daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 days 1-3) plus midostaurin 50 mg BID days 8-21 per the RATIFY protocol. Induction was complicated by febrile neutropenia with bilateral lower lobe pneumonia (presumed bacterial vs. fungal) managed with cefepime and micafungin, as well as grade 2 mucositis and midostaurin-related nausea. She achieved morphologic complete remission with MRD negativity by 8-color flow cytometry on day 28 bone marrow biopsy. Given her adverse-risk genomic profile, she proceeded directly to allogeneic SCT without consolidation chemotherapy.

She underwent 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor (MUD) allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation with myeloablative fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4) conditioning (pharmacokinetically dosed), GVHD prophylaxis with tacrolimus and short-course methotrexate, and CMV prophylaxis with letermovir. The donor was a 32-year-old CMV-seropositive male. CD34+ cell dose was 5.2 x 10^6/kg. Post-transplant course was notable for grade 3 mucositis requiring TPN and morphine PCA (resolved by day +18), transient tacrolimus-associated nephrotoxicity (creatinine peaked at 1.4 mg/dL, resolved), and subsequent development of mild chronic GVHD affecting skin (lichenoid papules, biopsy-confirmed, NIH grade 2 on histology) and GI tract (loose stools, NIH score 1). Chimerism at day +30 confirmed 98% donor (myeloid 99%, CD3 92%).

Chronic GVHD was managed with topical clobetasol for skin involvement (excellent response) and oral budesonide 3 mg TID for GI symptoms. She did not require systemic high-dose corticosteroids. Tacrolimus was gradually tapered and successfully discontinued at approximately month 11 without GVHD flare. Budesonide was discontinued at month 9. All prophylactic antimicrobials (letermovir, acyclovir, fluconazole, TMP-SMX) were discontinued per protocol at the 1-year mark. She has remained in morphologic and molecular complete remission with full donor chimerism on all bone marrow assessments through the 12-month biopsy (FLT3-ITD undetectable by qPCR at sensitivity 0.001%; DNMT3A R882H undetectable by ddPCR; RUNX1 frameshift undetectable by NGS deep sequencing; donor chimerism 99.8%).

Today, at 18 months post-transplant, she presents for routine surveillance. Since her last visit (3 months ago), she reports no new constitutional symptoms, no fevers, no night sweats, no unintentional weight loss, and no bleeding. She has resumed full-time teaching at her high school and reports her energy and stamina are at or near her pre-illness baseline. She does note mild ongoing skin dryness on her forearms, which she attributes to residual changes from prior GVHD; there are no new papules or rash. Bowel habits are normal. She denies dysphagia, odynophagia, oral dryness (previously noted, now improved), or ocular symptoms.

She is currently on no immunosuppression. The only regular medications are levothyroxine 75 mcg daily (pre-existing hypothyroidism, dose stable) and low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily.

This 48-year-old female presented with de novo acute myeloid leukemia with leukocytosis (WBC 87,400/uL), severe anemia (hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL), thrombocytopenia (platelets 32,000/uL), gingival hyperplasia, and circulating blasts (~40%) on peripheral smear. Immunophenotype by flow cytometry: CD34+, CD117+, CD13+, CD33+, MPO+, HLA-DR+, CD7 dim, CD19-, CD3- — consistent with AML, NOS. Conventional karyotype: 46,XX (normal female karyotype, 20/20 metaphases). FISH panel negative for t(8;21), inv(16), and t(15;17). This NGS study was performed on the diagnostic bone marrow aspirate specimen for comprehensive molecular profiling.
