━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

FINDING 1 — PATHOGENIC / CLINICALLY ACTIONABLE
Gene: FLT3
Alteration: Internal Tandem Duplication (ITD), Exon 14
Nucleotide Change: 33-base pair in-frame insertion within the juxtamembrane domain (c.1771_1772ins33)
Protein Effect: In-frame insertion, juxtamembrane domain
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 40.2%
FLT3-ITD Allelic Ratio: 0.68 (HIGH; threshold for high allelic ratio: ≥0.5)
Classification: Pathogenic
Clinical Significance: FLT3-ITD mutations occur in approximately 25-30% of AML cases and are among the most clinically impactful alterations in AML. This mutation results in constitutive, ligand-independent activation of the FLT3 receptor tyrosine kinase, driving proliferative signaling through RAS/MAPK, PI3K/AKT, and STAT5 pathways. A high FLT3-ITD allelic ratio (≥0.5) is independently associated with inferior complete remission rates, higher relapse risk, and significantly worse overall survival compared to low allelic ratio. In the NPM1-unmutated setting (as observed here), high-allelic-ratio FLT3-ITD confers Adverse risk classification per ELN 2022 guidelines. This mutation also predicts for clinical benefit from FLT3 inhibitor therapy; midostaurin (RATIFY trial, NEJM 2017) demonstrated improved event-free and overall survival when added to standard 7+3 induction in FLT3-mutated AML.
Note: FLT3 tyrosine kinase domain (TKD) mutation D835 was NOT detected (see negative findings). Co-occurrence with DNMT3A and TET2 (see below) defines a leukemogenic cluster associated with myeloid transformation.

FINDING 2 — PATHOGENIC
Gene: DNMT3A
Alteration: Missense mutation
Nucleotide Change: c.2645G>A
Protein Change: p.Arg882His (R882H)
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 48.1%
Chromosomal Location: Chr2q23.3
Classification: Pathogenic
Clinical Significance: The DNMT3A p.R882H hotspot mutation is the most frequent DNMT3A alteration in AML, accounting for approximately 60% of all DNMT3A mutations in this disease. DNMT3A encodes a de novo DNA methyltransferase critical for epigenetic regulation of hematopoietic gene expression. The R882H mutation acts through a dominant-negative mechanism, reducing DNMT3A enzymatic activity by approximately 80% and resulting in focal hypomethylation of CpG sites, particularly at hematopoietic stem cell-associated loci (e.g., HOX gene clusters). DNMT3A R882H is found at high VAF (~50%), consistent with its role as an early or founder event. Importantly, this mutation has been described as a CHIP (clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential) lesion and can persist as a pre-leukemic clone; its presence in the context of AML carries adverse prognostic implications. Co-mutation with FLT3-ITD is well-documented and synergistic: DNMT3A R882H establishes an epigenetically permissive state that potentiates FLT3-driven leukemic transformation. The high VAF (48.1%) suggests this mutation resides in the founding clone or a very early branch. Of note, this mutation is frequently detectable even in morphologic remission and does not constitute MRD positivity per current consensus.

FINDING 3 — PATHOGENIC
Gene: RUNX1
Alteration: Frameshift mutation
Nucleotide Change: c.871dupA (duplication leading to frameshift)
Protein Change: p.Ser291fs*16 (S291fs*16)
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 43.7%
Chromosomal Location: Chr21q22.12
Classification: Pathogenic
Clinical Significance: RUNX1 (Runt-related transcription factor 1; formerly AML1) encodes the alpha subunit of the CBF (core binding factor) transcription complex, which is essential for definitive hematopoiesis. This frameshift insertion results in loss of the transactivation domain and premature termination at position 306, generating a truncated protein predicted to function as a dominant-negative inhibitor of the remaining wild-type RUNX1 allele. RUNX1 mutations are found in approximately 10-15% of AML cases and carry significant prognostic weight. Under ELN 2022 classification, RUNX1 mutations confer Adverse risk when present without NPM1 mutation (as in this patient). RUNX1 mutations impair myeloid differentiation and augment self-renewal of leukemic progenitors. The co-occurrence of RUNX1 mutation with FLT3-ITD (high allelic ratio) and DNMT3A R882H in an NPM1-unmutated background constitutes a particularly high-risk molecular profile. The VAF of 43.7% is consistent with a clonal mutation in the dominant leukemic clone, potentially present in most blast cells given the high blast burden. This combination has been associated with primary resistance to standard induction chemotherapy and underscores the rationale for allogeneic stem cell transplantation in first complete remission.

FINDING 4 — PATHOGENIC
Gene: TET2
Alteration: Nonsense mutation
Nucleotide Change: c.2746C>T
Protein Change: p.Gln916* (Q916*)
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 22.4%
Chromosomal Location: Chr4q24
Classification: Pathogenic
Clinical Significance: TET2 encodes a dioxygenase that catalyzes the conversion of 5-methylcytosine (5mC) to 5-hydroxymethylcytosine (5hmC), an essential step in the active DNA demethylation pathway. This nonsense mutation at codon 916 introduces a premature stop codon, resulting in loss of TET2 enzymatic function and abnormal DNA hypermethylation at target loci. TET2 mutations are highly recurrent in both CHIP and myeloid malignancies; they are found in approximately 7-23% of AML cases. The lower VAF (22.4%) compared to the FLT3-ITD (40.2%) and DNMT3A (48.1%) alleles suggests this mutation is likely subclonal — present in a subset of leukemic cells and potentially representing late clonal evolution or a minor subclone. Alternatively, loss of heterozygosity at 4q24 could modulate the apparent VAF. TET2 loss-of-function cooperates with DNMT3A deficiency to create additive epigenetic dysregulation in leukemic stem cells. TET2 mutations have also been linked to hypersensitivity to hypomethylating agent therapy; this information may be relevant for future treatment decisions in the event of relapse.

FINDING 5 — VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (VUS)
Gene: WT1
Alteration: Missense mutation
Nucleotide Change: c.1385G>A
Protein Change: p.Arg462Gln (R462Q)
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 18.9%
Chromosomal Location: Chr11p13
Classification: Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS)
Clinical Significance: WT1 (Wilms Tumor 1) encodes a zinc finger transcription factor involved in hematopoietic differentiation. WT1 mutations occur in approximately 8-10% of AML cases, predominantly as missense or frameshift mutations in exons 7 and 9. The p.R462Q substitution affects the zinc finger DNA-binding domain (ZF4); functional studies of this specific variant are limited, which precludes definitive pathogenicity classification at this time. WT1 mutations have been reported to be associated with adverse outcomes in normal-karyotype AML and may be associated with resistance to standard chemotherapy, though findings across studies are heterogeneous. WT1 overexpression (separate from mutation) is used as a quantitative MRD marker in AML. The subclonal VAF (18.9%) places this mutation in a late subclone. Correlation with other molecular findings and clinical context is recommended.

FINDING 6 — LIKELY PATHOGENIC
Gene: PTPN11
Alteration: Missense mutation
Nucleotide Change: c.226G>A
Protein Change: p.Glu76Lys (E76K)
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 8.3%
Chromosomal Location: Chr12q24.13
Classification: Likely Pathogenic
Clinical Significance: PTPN11 encodes SHP-2 (Src homology-2 domain-containing protein tyrosine phosphatase-2), a positive regulator of the RAS/MAPK signaling pathway. The p.E76K mutation is a recurrent gain-of-function mutation that activates SHP-2, resulting in enhanced RAS-MAPK and PI3K-AKT signaling. PTPN11 activating mutations are more commonly associated with juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia (JMML) and Noonan syndrome but are found in approximately 2-5% of AML cases. This mutation is present at a low VAF (8.3%), consistent with a minor subclone, possibly representing late clonal evolution or minor resistant cell population. RAS pathway activation through PTPN11 may contribute to proliferative signaling and could represent a cooperative lesion with FLT3-ITD. Notably, no NRAS or KRAS mutations were identified (see negative findings below), making this the sole RAS pathway activating event in this specimen.

METHODOLOGY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
DNA and RNA were extracted from bone marrow aspirate using standard protocols. Library preparation utilized a hybrid-capture approach covering all exons of 523 clinically relevant hematologic malignancy genes, as well as intronic sequences spanning known fusion breakpoints. FLT3-ITD detection employed a dedicated amplicon-based high-depth sequencing assay with fragment length analysis as an orthogonal confirmatory method. Allelic ratio for FLT3-ITD was calculated as ITD-containing reads divided by total (WT + ITD) reads. Variant calling employed validated bioinformatics pipelines with matched germline reference (buccal swab). Somatic variants were confirmed as somatic by absence in matched normal. All variants reported at VAF ≥ 3% in tumor regions with coverage ≥ 500x. Copy number analysis was performed using normalization against a panel of normals. Fusion detection utilized both DNA-level breakpoint capture and RNA-seq-based junction read analysis.

Her primary care physician obtained a CBC that demonstrated a leukocytosis with WBC 87,400/uL, hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL, and platelets 32,000/uL. A staging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrated mild hepatosplenomegaly (spleen 14.2 cm, liver 17.1 cm) and small bilateral pleural effusions. No discrete tumor masses. Brain MRI showed no leptomeningeal enhancement or parenchymal lesions, arguing against CNS involvement.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Chen is a 48-year-old woman presenting with de novo AML with a high-risk molecular profile. She has no prior hematologic disorder or antecedent myelodysplastic syndrome. Her disease is characterized by high-allelic-ratio FLT3-ITD in the context of NPM1-unmutated normal-karyotype AML, with co-occurring DNMT3A R882H and RUNX1 frameshift mutations — a combination that places her firmly in the ELN 2022 Adverse risk category and is associated with a high risk of relapse even after achieving complete remission with standard induction. There is no evidence of CNS involvement, no leukostasis clinically, and she has adequate organ function and ECOG PS 1, making her an appropriate candidate for intensive induction chemotherapy.

1. INDUCTION CHEMOTHERAPY: We will proceed with standard 7+3 induction chemotherapy: cytarabine 100 mg/m² per day via continuous intravenous infusion on days 1 through 7, plus daunorubicin 60 mg/m² IV on days 1, 2, and 3. Given her FLT3-ITD mutation with high allelic ratio, we will add midostaurin 50 mg PO twice daily on days 8 through 21 per the RATIFY trial protocol (Stone et al., NEJM 2017), which demonstrated improved OS and EFS when midostaurin was added to 7+3 induction in FLT3-mutated AML. Chemotherapy to begin today following administration of the second unit of packed red blood cells and following pre-hydration for tumor lysis prophylaxis. LVEF confirmed adequate at 62% by pre-treatment echo, permitting full-dose anthracycline.

2. SUPPORTIVE CARE AND TUMOR LYSIS PROPHYLAXIS: Allopurinol 300 mg PO daily initiated. Aggressive IV hydration per protocol. Uric acid and electrolytes monitored every 6-8 hours for the first 48-72 hours. Transfusion thresholds: pRBC for hemoglobin <8 g/dL, platelets for count <10,000/uL (or <20,000/uL with active bleeding/fever). Anti-emetics: ondansetron plus dexamethasone per standard protocol; midostaurin-related nausea is anticipated and additional agents (prochlorperazine, lorazepam) will be available as needed. Growth factor support (G-CSF) will be withheld during and immediately following induction per standard practice, as it may interfere with leukemia clearance.

3. INFECTION PROPHYLAXIS: Antifungal prophylaxis with micafungin will be initiated at onset of neutropenia. Acyclovir for HSV prophylaxis. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for PJP prophylaxis will be deferred until count recovery per standard protocol. Surveillance blood cultures if febrile. Empiric broad-spectrum gram-negative coverage (e.g., cefepime) for febrile neutropenia episodes per institutional protocol.

4. TRANSPLANT PLANNING — INITIATED NOW: Given her adverse ELN risk profile (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, NPM1-unmutated, RUNX1 mutation, DNMT3A mutation), we strongly recommend proceeding directly to allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (alloHSCT) in first complete remission, should she respond to induction. The decision to forego consolidation chemotherapy in favor of expedited transplant is appropriate in this risk category. HLA typing has been sent today on the patient. Consent obtained to contact her one full sibling (age 52, male, healthy) for HLA typing. Simultaneously, we will initiate an unrelated donor search through the National Marrow Donor Program (NMDP/Be The Match) registry, given the possibility that the sibling may not be a full match. The search will proceed in parallel with induction.

5. BONE MARROW RESPONSE ASSESSMENT: Bone marrow biopsy will be scheduled for day 28 post-induction start to assess for complete remission. MRD assessment by 8-color flow cytometry will be performed at that time, in addition to cytogenetics and molecular PCR for FLT3-ITD. If CR with MRD negativity is achieved, we will proceed expeditiously to transplant planning.

6. ONGOING MONITORING: CBC and comprehensive metabolic panel with LDH daily during induction. Coagulation studies (PT, PTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer) every 48 hours given mild coagulopathy at baseline, to monitor for DIC. Tacrolimus/immunosuppression not yet applicable but relevant post-transplant. Thyroid function monitoring given levothyroxine use and anticipated physiologic stress.

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL AND FAMILY SUPPORT: This is a devastating diagnosis for a previously healthy 48-year-old. I spent considerable time discussing the diagnosis, the treatment plan, expected course of hospitalization (minimum 4-5 weeks for induction), risks and expected complications (febrile neutropenia, mucositis, transfusion dependence), and the overall treatment strategy including transplant planning. Ms. Chen is a highly educated individual who asked thoughtful questions and demonstrated excellent understanding of the information discussed. Her husband was present and was also deeply engaged. Social work has been consulted. A care conference with her family will be arranged. Fertility counseling was briefly discussed — given the urgency of treatment and the myeloablative conditioning that will follow, fertility preservation is unfortunately not feasible at this juncture without delaying potentially life-saving therapy. This was acknowledged by the patient.

8. FINANCIAL COUNSELING: Financial counseling and insurance authorization processes for midostaurin have been initiated. Patient assistance programs noted as available if needed.

Overall prognosis: Ms. Chen has high-risk AML. With intensive induction and consolidative alloHSCT in first CR, long-term disease-free survival is achievable, though her ELN Adverse risk profile with FLT3-ITD AR >0.5 and co-occurring RUNX1 mutation confers meaningful relapse risk. The goal remains cure. We have communicated this clearly and honestly to the patient and her family.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue, present but stable. No new fevers since defervescence on day +15. No night sweats.
- HEENT: Oral mucositis improving, currently manageable without PCA (transitioned to oral morphine IR as needed). Gingival hyperplasia markedly improved compared to presentation -- gingival oozing has resolved with blast clearance from the peripheral blood. Mild xerostomia.
- Pulmonary: No shortness of breath at rest. Mild exertional dyspnea with ambulation to bathroom; attributed to anemia and deconditioning. No worsening cough.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or peripheral edema.
- GI: Nausea present, improved on prochlorperazine. Tolerating small amounts of oral intake -- soft diet and nutritional supplements. Loose stools (1-2 per day), likely antibiotic-related. No hematochezia or melena.
- GU: Adequate urine output. No dysuria. No hematuria.
- Neuro: No headache, visual changes, or focal neurologic symptoms. No signs of leukostasis (WBC now at nadir, effectively zero blasts in periphery).
- Musculoskeletal: Generalized weakness and myalgia consistent with prolonged hospitalization and cytopenia.
- Skin: Petechiae noted early in hospitalization have resolved. Mild mucositis-related lip dryness.
- Hematologic: Active cytopenia as expected. Receiving transfusion support per thresholds (pRBC for Hgb <8 g/dL, platelets for count <10,000/uL or active bleeding).

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs: Temperature 37.1 degrees C (afebrile), heart rate 88 bpm (regular), blood pressure 108/68 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, O2 saturation 97% on room air. Weight 63.2 kg (down approximately 2.5 kg from admission).

General: Alert and oriented x3. Pale-appearing but in no acute distress. Fatigued. Cooperative and conversant.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Conjunctival pallor present. Oropharynx with mild residual mucositis -- scattered areas of erythema and mild pseudomembrane formation along the buccal mucosa, improved from peak. No active bleeding from gingiva. Gingival hyperplasia has decreased appreciably compared to admission, consistent with blast clearance.

Neck: No cervical lymphadenopathy palpable.

Lungs: Breath sounds are coarse bilaterally at the bases, slightly improved compared to day +10. No wheezing. No egophony or frank consolidation on auscultation today. Breathing comfortably at rest.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1/S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Abdomen: Soft, mild tenderness to deep palpation in the left upper quadrant. Spleen tip palpable approximately 2.5 to 3 cm below the left costal margin -- decreased compared to the 4 cm noted at admission, reflecting treatment response. Liver edge no longer palpable below the right costal margin.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No petechiae or purpura visible today. Ecchymoses fading from venipuncture sites. Peripheral IV access intact and functioning bilaterally.

Neurologic: Alert. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Gait not formally assessed today (patient using bedside commode due to fatigue/orthostasis risk).

Skin: Pale. Fading ecchymoses. No new rash. No central line site erythema or discharge (PICC line right upper arm).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (Today, Day +14):

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 0.2 k/uL (nadir; markedly decreased from 87,400/uL at presentation -- representing effective blast and leukemia clearance from peripheral blood)
- Differential: ANC 0/uL (absolute neutropenia), lymphocytes predominant on smear (minimal)
- Hemoglobin: 8.4 g/dL (transfusion-supported; s/p 1 unit pRBCs earlier today per threshold)
- Hematocrit: 25.3%
- MCV: 93 fL
- Platelets: 9,000/uL (transfusion-supported; 1 unit pooled platelets transfused this morning for count below threshold)

Cumulative transfusion requirements during this hospitalization: 6 units packed red blood cells and 12 units platelets to date.

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 136 mEq/L
- Potassium: 3.8 mEq/L
- Chloride: 100 mEq/L
- CO2: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 14 mg/dL
- Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL (stable baseline)
- Glucose: 118 mg/dL (mild elevation, likely stress-related/steroid-related, monitoring)
- Total Bilirubin: 1.2 mg/dL
- Direct Bilirubin: 0.4 mg/dL
- ALT: 38 U/L (improving from 58 U/L earlier this week; trending down)
- AST: 32 U/L (improving from 45 U/L; trending down)
- Alkaline Phosphatase: 72 U/L
- Total Protein: 6.1 g/dL
- Albumin: 3.2 g/dL (mildly low, consistent with nutritional depletion and inflammation)

LDH: 198 U/L (down from 1,240 U/L at admission; significant improvement consistent with blast lysis and response to therapy).

Uric acid: 4.8 mg/dL (normalized on allopurinol; no evidence of ongoing tumor lysis).

Micafungin level: not routinely monitored; drug continued at 150 mg IV daily.

Blood Cultures (drawn day +10, at febrile episode): No growth in 5 days x 4 sets (2 peripheral, 2 from PICC). No bacteremia identified.

Urine culture (obtained day +10): No growth.

Beta-D-glucan (sent day +11): Pending (awaiting result; will guide antifungal duration if positive).

CMV PCR (sent day +12): Negative (no CMV viremia detected).

---

IMAGING RESULTS:

CT Chest (day +10, obtained at time of febrile neutropenia): Bilateral lower lobe consolidations, right greater than left, with associated air bronchograms, compatible with infectious pneumonia. No cavitary lesions to suggest aspergillosis, though halo sign was absent -- cannot exclude early fungal infection. No pleural effusions significantly worsened from prior. No pneumothorax.

No new imaging today; clinical stability does not warrant repeat CT at this time.

---

ASSESSMENT:

Ms. Chen is a 48-year-old female with de novo AML (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, DNMT3A/RUNX1/TET2 co-mutations, ELN 2022 Adverse risk) now at day +14 following 7+3 induction (cytarabine/daunorubicin) plus midostaurin. She is currently in the expected aplastic nadir. Key assessment points:

1. AML, in aplastic nadir following induction -- day +14: The patient is progressing appropriately through the expected post-induction nadir. WBC is 0.2 k/uL with ANC of zero and circulating blasts are absent from the peripheral smear, consistent with effective marrow ablation by cytotoxic induction. The dramatic drop in LDH from over 1,200 at admission to 198 today is reassuring for significant leukemia cell kill. Morphologic response will be formally assessed by bone marrow biopsy at day +28 per protocol. We remain cautiously optimistic given the depth of aplasia and absence of circulating blasts. This is the expected and necessary nadir window post-cytarabine/anthracycline.

2. Febrile neutropenia / presumed pneumonia, improving: Patient has been afebrile for the past 4 days on cefepime and micafungin. Lung exam is improving, though the CT changes at the bases persist. We are continuing the current antibiotic regimen. Beta-D-glucan results are pending; if elevated, we will continue micafungin for an extended course with further workup (galactomannan, possible CT follow-up). If negative in the appropriate clinical context, we can reassess deescalation of antifungal coverage after completing a full 14-day course of cefepime for presumed bacterial pneumonia.

3. Midostaurin-related nausea/vomiting, grade 2: Ongoing but improving with the addition of prochlorperazine. Midostaurin 50 mg PO BID continues through day +21 per RATIFY protocol. Dose reduction is not planned at this time given the importance of FLT3 inhibition during induction and the manageable nature of the side effect with antiemetic augmentation. We will reassess if symptoms worsen or if patient cannot maintain adequate oral intake.

4. Oral mucositis, improving: Peak mucositis has passed. Transitioning from PCA to as-needed oral morphine IR. Patient is beginning to tolerate small amounts of oral intake (soft diet, nutritional supplements). Will continue close monitoring of nutritional status; dietitian has been following. Albumin 3.2 g/dL reflects inflammatory and nutritional depletion; no TPN indicated at this time given improving oral tolerance.

5. Transaminase elevation, resolving: ALT and AST have been trending downward toward normal range. Most likely etiology remains midostaurin hepatotoxicity vs. micafungin, though other causes (hepatic congestion from prior hepatomegaly, ongoing infectious/inflammatory process) cannot be fully excluded. Will continue to monitor LFTs every 2-3 days.

6. Anemia and thrombocytopenia, transfusion-dependent: Counts are at nadir as expected. Transfusing per institutional thresholds (pRBC trigger Hgb <8 g/dL; platelet trigger <10,000/uL or bleeding). Total transfusion support to date: 6 units pRBC and 12 units platelets. Cumulative transfusion exposure will be relevant for alloimmunization surveillance going forward. No signs of platelet refractoriness at this time.

7. Hypothyroidism: Levothyroxine continued at home dose throughout hospitalization. No evidence of hypothyroid exacerbation.

8. Long-term planning: Given her Adverse-risk molecular profile (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, RUNX1 mutation, DNMT3A mutation), allogeneic stem cell transplantation in first complete remission remains the plan. HLA typing has been sent on the patient and her one full sibling. Unrelated donor search has been initiated concurrently. Transplant center has been notified and a consultation is anticipated post-remission. No consolidation chemotherapy is anticipated prior to transplant, per our multidisciplinary discussion. Bridge therapy options (e.g., midostaurin continuation) will be discussed at that time.

---

PLAN:

1. Continue neutropenic precautions: strict hand hygiene, HEPA-filtered room, low-microbial diet, monitoring for fever q4h.

2. Continue cefepime 2g IV q8h -- currently on day 5 of planned 14-day course for presumed bacterial pneumonia.

3. Continue micafungin 150 mg IV daily for antifungal coverage. Await beta-D-glucan result; reassess duration accordingly. Galactomannan will be sent if beta-D-glucan returns positive.

4. Continue midostaurin 50 mg PO BID through day +21. Continue prochlorperazine PRN for nausea. Encourage small frequent meals.

5. Transfusion support: transfuse pRBCs for Hgb <8 g/dL; platelets for count <10,000/uL. Pre-medicate with acetaminophen and diphenhydramine per policy.

6. Continue oral morphine IR (4 mg q4h PRN) for mucositis pain. Re-evaluate with each shift.

7. Recheck CBC, CMP, LDH tomorrow (day +15). LFTs to be repeated in 2 days given prior mild elevation.

8. Await CMV PCR and beta-D-glucan results; review and act accordingly.

9. Bone marrow biopsy and aspirate scheduled for day +28 for morphologic response assessment and MRD evaluation. This will determine next steps in treatment, including timing of allogeneic SCT.

10. Continue levothyroxine at pre-admission dose.

11. Nutritional support: Continue soft diet as tolerated. Nutritional supplement shakes BID. Dietitian to follow. No TPN required at this time.

12. PT/OT consult placed for deconditioning -- progressive mobilization as tolerated, within safe limits given thrombocytopenia (fall precautions maintained).

13. Psychosocial: Social worker following; patient and family have been provided with educational resources regarding AML, the post-induction waiting period, and what to expect from bone marrow biopsy results. Husband is present daily and is engaged in care planning.

14. Continue patient and family education regarding signs/symptoms of early relapse, infection, and bleeding to report to nursing staff.

No changes in code status required -- patient is full code.

Total time spent in patient care activities and documentation: approximately 45 minutes.
